title: Alternatives to Driving: Building a Realistic Transportation Plan
summary: Giving up driving is easier to accept when there's already a real plan in place for getting around — not just a vague promise of help.
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Alternatives to Driving: Building a Realistic Transportation Plan | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Giving up driving is easier to accept when there's already a real plan in place for getting around — not just a vague promise of help.
whatYouNeedToKnow: A mix of options (family, rideshare, senior transportation services) usually works better than relying on one.
Many areas have senior-specific transportation programs that are underused.
Practicing the alternative before it's the only option reduces resistance.

paragraphs: Rather than relying on one solution, a combination — family members for regular errands, rideshare apps for spontaneous trips, and local senior transportation services for medical appointments — tends to hold up better over time than any single option alone.

Many communities have senior transportation programs through local agencies on aging that are significantly underused simply because families don't know they exist. It's worth a quick search or call to the local Area Agency on Aging to find out what's available.

nextSteps: Research local senior transportation and rideshare options before the driving conversation happens.
Set up rideshare accounts and test them together while driving is still an option.
Identify a small circle of people who can help with regular errands.
disclaimer: General planning guidance — availability of specific programs varies significantly by location.
